summary: Rustinel (GitHub Repo) Karib0u's Rust-based user-mode EDR uses ETW for Windows kernel telemetry, normalizing data into Sysmon schemas for Sigma, YARA, and atomic IOC matching. Note: While the README claims a v1.0 release with Linux eBPF support, the repository remains an Alpha Windows-only tool (v0.3.1) lacking eBPF code.

title: OpenSSH Flaw Allowing Full Root Shell Access Lurked for 15 Years
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 2 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2Fj2Qv4o/1/0100019dd4358207-84f7e3c0-d39a-4a57-a42b-1efb325a77d8-000000/eF8l2ww8REISU4r-BRaC2i8Z-3B60Ue_uwZ5iHmdDV4=452
summary: OpenSSH Flaw Allowing Full Root Shell Access Lurked for 15 Years (2 minute read) CVE-2026-35414 affects OpenSSH versions from the past 15 years. A code reuse error allows commas in SSH certificate principals to be parsed as list separators. If a certificate contains "deploy,root" as a principal, OpenSSH splits on the comma and grants root access. The attack leaves no authentication failure in logs and researchers created a working exploit in twenty minutes. OpenSSH 10.3 patches the flaw.

title: Batch API is terrible for one agent. It might be great for a fleet
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Feran.sandler.co.il%2Fpost%2F2026-04-27-batch-api-is-terrible-for-one-agent%2F%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd43fd7ba-2b251623-4fe1-44a4-99ee-bf2819091b58-000000/iTYNNlzxSkOELiaJ9uBU5hcJpfpSBx88MUsENbtYaSw=452
summary: Batch API is terrible for one agent. It might be great for a fleet (6 minute read) Batch API offers a 50% discount but adds latency, making it less suitable for single-agent use. For fleets of agents where multiple requests can be pooled, the batching approach becomes economically viable. Optimal usage involves routing slower, costlier models through batches, while employing faster models via synchronous paths, potentially managed by intelligent proxies like the developing LunaRoute.

title: GPT 5.5: The System Card
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 20 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fthezvi.wordpress.com%2F2026%2F04%2F27%2Fgpt-5-5-the-system-card%2F%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd43fd7ba-2b251623-4fe1-44a4-99ee-bf2819091b58-000000/UYeTrNZX-Dp36mRzUolvn_OAEW52WpuxlfrlVbs2V8c=452
summary: GPT 5.5: The System Card (20 minute read) GPT-5.5 is a solid improvement and is competitive with Claude Opus. It seems to be better for factual queries, web searches, and straightforward, well-specified requests, while Claude Opus excels in more open-ended or interpretive purposes. The model is unlikely to pose new big risks, and its alignment seems similar to that of previous models. This post examines the system card for GPT-5.5.

title: Compressing AI vectors to 2–4 bits per number without losing accuracy
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 54 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Farkaung.github.io%2Finteractive-turboquant%2F%3Futm_source=tldrai/1/0100019dd43fd7ba-2b251623-4fe1-44a4-99ee-bf2819091b58-000000/neruG57lo0lql8zLEHPoF_tGDTwhCoKB1GSoI4O205A=452
summary: Compressing AI vectors to 2–4 bits per number without losing accuracy (54 minute read) TurboQuant compresses each coordinate in large tables of high-dimensional vectors to 2-4 bits with provably near-optimal distortion, no memory overhead for scale factors, and no training or calibration. It is between four and six orders of magnitude faster than the alternatives at 4-bit indexing, with higher recall as well. This page explains how TurboQuant works.

title: Recursive Language Models, clearly explained

summary: Recursive Language Models, clearly explained (3 minute read) MIT researchers have introduced Recursive Language Models (RLMs) to solve "context rot," a phenomenon where large language models experience reasoning degradation when processing massive context windows, even if they excel at basic retrieval tasks. Instead of forcing a model to ingest an entire document at once, an RLM loads the context into a Python REPL runtime memory slot.

title: The Moat or the Commons
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-28, Reading time: 10 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FTGeuCw/1/0100019dd43fd7ba-2b251623-4fe1-44a4-99ee-bf2819091b58-000000/IbK_lDJQhsSSM27Ke0R5Zg7eCzdJqSDKS9Ge1pJKArc=452
summary: The Moat or the Commons (10 minute read) American AI was financed on the bet that frontier models would be the next great monopoly business. That assumption is now breaking as open weight models are commoditizing the capability that the American capital paid-for moat was supposed to protect. The gap between the open frontier and the closed frontier is closing. The question will be whether countries choose to subsidize the private moat or the open commons.

summary: Curious cases of financial engineering in biotech (32 minute read) Finance doesn't really make drug development easier. However, it does make failure more survivable. The industry being willing to fund biology that's expensive and likely to fail could result in more discoveries that change the world.

title: The Brave New World of AI Markets
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinks.tldrnewsletter.com%2FYsSYj6/1/0100019dd9249a78-66c9acfa-e59d-4d83-b1f6-96ceb00feaac-000000/hX5xz0kwTaqPrGdCHxMPC6woNe-oJXrlXI2G9WCyn6U=452
summary: The Brave New World of AI Markets (5 minute read) It is naive to assume that the Total Addressable Market (TAM) for artificial intelligence will simply equal the current size of the global human labor market. This post proposes a new framework for sizing AI markets based on the classic economic equation of Price multiplied by Quantity (TAM = Human Labor TAM × Price Compression Factor × Volume Expansion Factor).

title: Direction Over Speed
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 7 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.leahtharin.com%2Fp%2Fdirection-over-speed%3Futm_source=tldrfounders/1/0100019dd9249a78-66c9acfa-e59d-4d83-b1f6-96ceb00feaac-000000/fZ_IyO1JoF5OonKq-_SMvQUjeWOBCPvbZMgBuW202gU=452
summary: Direction Over Speed (7 minute read) For most of software's history, you could fake productivity by shipping a lot. AI broke that cover. Prototypes are cheap, specs write themselves, and shipping looks the same whether you're building the right thing or the wrong one. Smaller teams of four or five, with the PM closer to the work, especially on AI features where "is this good" can't be answered by a metric. The model can build the thing for you. It still can't tell you why anyone should care.

title: Shipped Isn't Solved
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwildfirelabs.substack.com%2Fp%2Fshipped-isnt-solved%3Futm_source=tldrfounders/1/0100019dd9249a78-66c9acfa-e59d-4d83-b1f6-96ceb00feaac-000000/LuFy5jxDr_eLyFDrrtcUtzqeEaAbty-sZAJRDkbt2VA=452
summary: Shipped Isn't Solved (5 minute read) A customer asked for a feature on a call. The team built it, shipped it fast, and moved on. Nobody really used it. Speed only helps if you understand the problem before you start building. Look at your last five shipped features and count how many customers were still using each one a month later.

title: Agent Auth: Why OAuth Wasn't Built for This
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 11 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.apideck.com%2Fblog%2Fagent-auth-oauth-ai-agents%23where-this-leaves-builders%3Futm_source=tldrit/1/0100019dd9285b20-7650f0b9-32fb-4b19-b5b5-ab86dded81ca-000000/nsy3WH5oOactSjd6-uhxMwW9_nUuPczeql8R5o0aJEM=452
summary: Agent Auth: Why OAuth Wasn't Built for This (11 minute read) Standard OAuth 2.0 fails to support AI agents that make runtime decisions and delegate tasks. title: Output isn't design
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-29, Reading time: 4 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Flinear.app%2Fnow%2Foutput-isn-t-design%3Futm_source=tldrdesign/1/0100019dd934fd98-551bb87b-a592-44e7-9728-f3d36824a554-000000/qnas5Y8_Ecjj7_LfzbF0Pr64KffhrHmyLOzJz2pz_pw=452
summary: Output isn't design (4 minute read) Design isn't about producing outputs but about deeply understanding a problem and achieving a good fit between form and context. Tools (including AI) can generate results quickly, but they don't replace the thinking required to resolve underlying complexities. Overreliance on AI risks creating polished yet fragile products, because real design value comes from the slow, iterative process that clarifies understanding—not just the final output.

summary: Can agents replace the search stack? (7 minute read) Giving agents basic search tools results in better quality answers. Encouraging the agent to explore further creates more improvements. Agentic search models can figure out how to surface relevant results, but the training data still dictates the models' sweet spots. Large language models can't evaluate what they don't know, and there's still no real way to compensate for their lack of knowledge.

title: A/B Testing Pitfalls: What Works and What Doesn't with Real Data
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 5 minute read, Links: http://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/http:%2F%2Fkdnuggets.com%2Fa-b-testing-pitfalls-what-works-and-what-doesnt-with-real-data%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019ddddcba37-0f8724c1-cfa9-4d11-a095-98e86c4491ee-000000/oW4LS5JtKpV2OkZdLRVFrfwLBG5fiZP5YVEGWBdhxz4=452
summary: A/B Testing Pitfalls: What Works and What Doesn't with Real Data (5 minute read) A/B testing failures are far more often caused by broken infrastructure and poor experimentation practices than by the ideas being tested. Common failures include Sample Ratio Mismatch (SRM) from bad randomization, early peeking that inflates false positives, insufficient statistical power, and optimizing the wrong metrics without guardrails, causing misleading results.

title: HOT Updates in Postgres
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 12 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fboringsql.com%2Fposts%2Fhot-updates%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdata/1/0100019ddddcba37-0f8724c1-cfa9-4d11-a095-98e86c4491ee-000000/ZBym8FDBScZ5NTZIq5uotrlxmDcHjxNSm7Gd4dpheXA=452
summary: HOT Updates in Postgres (12 minute read) HOT Updates in PostgreSQL is a clever storage optimization that allows UPDATEs on unindexed columns to avoid touching indexes entirely when the new tuple fits on the same page as the old one. Instead of creating new index entries, PostgreSQL marks the old tuple as HOT_UPDATED and places a HEAP_ONLY tuple on the same page, forming a chain that scans can follow, which reduces WAL traffic, index maintenance, and vacuuming overhead.

This simplifies ETL pipelines by automatically handling job lifecycle, schema evolution, and refreshes.

title: Evaluating Netflix Show Synopses with LLM-as-a-Judge
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 9 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fnetflixtechblog.com%2Fevaluating-netflix-show-synopses-with-llm-as-a-judge-6269251e6f28%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dde14bf47-7431359d-9783-4cb6-bb43-bb9e6ef45f62-000000/lVXu2Nt9rFc__k3vVHjcNA7UNFGgxsZ2PoMiWuLQlF4=452
summary: Evaluating Netflix Show Synopses with LLM-as-a-Judge (9 minute read) Netflix developed an LLM-as-a-Judge system to score show synopses on four quality dimensions, anchoring it to a golden set of roughly 600 expert-labeled examples built through a rigorous calibration process. This approach, which uses judges and techniques like tiered reasoning and consensus scoring, achieves 83–92% accuracy and allows them to flag and fix weak synopses weeks before a show ships.

title: The Last Software Engineer
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 7 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.epicproduct.engineer%2Fthe-last-software-engineer%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dde14bf47-7431359d-9783-4cb6-bb43-bb9e6ef45f62-000000/ewenc0_Ofs2wjCPYRpr8sFxoAnximCxfguopWshAm3A=452
summary: The Last Software Engineer (7 minute read) As AI agents automate implementation, software engineering value is shifting from writing code to exercising judgment about what to build and why. Product engineering now requires humans to own implementation consequences and user impact, making strategic decision-making the most durable skill for engineers.

title: The end of "Just ask Sarah"
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fsimme.dev%2Fposts%2Fthe-end-of-just-ask-sarah%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dde14bf47-7431359d-9783-4cb6-bb43-bb9e6ef45f62-000000/VapDS1SskfV5q9vDEp0tgGfPpUtVP9Y9yxU2DFjpN4s=452
summary: The end of "Just ask Sarah" (6 minute read) AI agents shift the role of docs from organizational memory for humans to execution context for machines. Unlike human engineers who can rely on unwritten institutional knowledge, agents only have their limited context window, forgetting information between sessions. This absence of durable intent leads agents to perpetuate patterns without understanding their underlying reasoning, resulting in "intent debt" and potentially incorrect decisions.

title: How to be Successful Interviewing for Big Tech
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 10 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fblog.postman.com%2Fhow-to-be-successful-interviewing-for-big-tech%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dde14bf47-7431359d-9783-4cb6-bb43-bb9e6ef45f62-000000/utNxK6ijex-jwUPoVo-AQDei048qB3q47XN8hbcdtfw=452
summary: How to be Successful Interviewing for Big Tech (10 minute read) Traditional Big Tech interviews, often focused on LeetCode problems, inaccurately assess an engineer's true capability to ship working software. The interview process, like at Postman, should be designed to mirror the actual job, prioritizing real-world relevance, the use of modern tools including AI, and collaborative problem-solving.

summary: Two foundation models built for agentic coding. title: Copy Fail
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fcopy.fail%2F%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dde14bf47-7431359d-9783-4cb6-bb43-bb9e6ef45f62-000000/oSlixmw7Bk3dRklJ67LcFK7O0roD_mx6TBjuoE_OFUA=452
summary: Copy Fail (6 minute read) "Copy Fail" (CVE-2026-31431) is a critical Linux kernel flaw that has existed since 2017. It allows unprivileged local users to gain root access via a simple Python script. This vulnerability poses a severe risk to multi-tenant environments like container clusters, requiring kernel updates or disabling the `algif_aead` module for mitigation.

title: I benchmarked caveman against two words
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 6 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.maxtaylor.me%2Farticles%2Fi-benchmarked-caveman-against-two-words%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dde14bf47-7431359d-9783-4cb6-bb43-bb9e6ef45f62-000000/IIKyMsye1m7r4Cy4wcswh5bAgapQ_N9iUVRj1m4GaEg=452
summary: I benchmarked caveman against two words (6 minute read) Benchmarks comparing the "Caveman" Claude code compression plugin against the simple prompt "be brief" found that the two-word prompt matched Caveman in both token reduction and output quality across most tested categories. While "be brief" is recommended for simple token reduction, Caveman is ideal for those needing predictable and structured output formats.

comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-04-30, Reading time: 7 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fwww.agwa.name%2Fblog%2Fpost%2Ffastcgi_is_the_better_protocol_for_reverse_proxies%3Futm_source=tldrdev/1/0100019dde14bf47-7431359d-9783-4cb6-bb43-bb9e6ef45f62-000000/LoK6Cl9GB4G2riK3H1qIwYqJI_TXgBlt5c92aBFCJDc=452

summary: Mini Shai Hulud and SAP Compromise (11 minute read) Attackers compromised SAP maintainer accounts to publish four malicious npm packages on April 29. summary: Netflix's TikTok-like vertical feed is finally here (2 minute read) Netflix's vertical feed, Clips, is rolling out to the US, the UK, Australia, Canada, India, Malaysia, Pakistan, the Philippines, and South Africa. Clips shows short clips from series, films, and specials tailored to users' tastes. It is designed to help people decide what to watch or play next. Netflix plans to expand Clips to include podcasts, live programming, and collections based on genres.

summary: Zuckerberg-backed Biohub bets $500M on AI biology (3 minute read) Mark Zuckerberg and Priscilla Chan's nonprofit, Biohub, is committing $500 million to help create better AI simulations of the human body. Biohub's long-term goal is to cure all human diseases through the intersection of AI and biology. It is betting that more data and compute will produce more useful models. Of the $500 million, Biohub will spend $400 million on its own work and $100 million to spur others.

title: Copy Fail: 732 Bytes to Root on Every Major Linux Distribution
comment: Source: TLDR Newsletter, Date: 2026-05-01, Reading time: 20 minute read, Links: https://tracking.tldrnewsletter.com/CL0/https:%2F%2Fxint.io%2Fblog%2Fcopy-fail-linux-distributions%3Futm_source=tldrnewsletter/1/0100019de310acdf-3ae8ecaf-8d97-4f6a-82c2-4c955fd7b407-000000/Y_LU7BoulFeQXdQ_OjDWVtBB_CcXrtNAMVXwPcfc7FI=452
summary: Copy Fail: 732 Bytes to Root on Every Major Linux Distribution (20 minute read) Copy Fail is a logic bug in the Linux kernel's authencesn cryptographic template that lets unprivileged users trigger a deterministic, controlled 4-byte write into the page cache of any readable file on the system. It can be exploited using a single 732-byte Python script to obtain root on essentially all Linux distributions shipped since 2017. Its discovery was AI-assisted. A patch for the bug is available.
